result: I’ve been thinking about late-night convenience stores, the ones glowing like aquariums in the middle of a dark suburb. There’s a hush inside, the hum of freezers louder than any customer, and the clerks—no matter the city—lean as if they’ve been carved into the counter since 1993. Last Tuesday I wandered into one for band-aids and left with nothing but a single lychee ramune that tasted like someone else’s childhood. The drink itself was mediocre, yet I carried the glass marble home in my pocket, rolling it between fingers through two bus transfers, afraid it would disappear the way certain hours do. I keep it on my desk now, beside a dead succulent and a library book I’ve renewed six times, proof that sometimes we purchase small, bright things just to confirm we’re still movable in the world. The marble’s milky swirl catches 3 a.m. lamplight like stored moon, reminding me that being open at odd hours is a form of kindness, whether you’re a store or a person. I like to imagine the clerk restocking instant noodles, humming something his grandmother played on vinyl, believing no one notices. I noticed. The bell above the door sounded like a tiny cathedral, announcing that I, too, am still unlocked for business, willing to trade coins for wonder, willing to carry something breakable a little farther into the night, all of us fragile and fizzing, waiting for someone thirsty to arrive.

The drink itself was mediocre, yet I carried the glass marble home in my pocket, rolling it between fingers through two bus transfers, afraid it would disappear the way certain hours do. I keep it on my desk now, beside a dead succulent and a library book I’ve renewed six times, proof that sometimes we purchase small, bright things just to confirm we’re still movable in the world. The marble’s milky swirl catches 3 a.m. lamplight like stored moon, reminding me that being open at odd hours is a form of kindness, whether you’re a store or a person. I like to imagine the clerk restocking instant noodles, humming something his grandmother played on vinyl, believing no one notices. I noticed. The bell above the door sounded like a tiny cathedral, announcing that I, too, am still unlocked for business, willing to trade coins for wonder, willing to carry something breakable a little farther into the night, all of us fragile and fizzing, waiting for someone thirsty to arrive.
